title: Hávamál
stanzas: He little knows who hath never learned,
That ale and gold are an evil pair;
More men are lost through the drink of ale
Than ever are saved by the yellow gold.
On the threshold tall thus spoke Thritharkur;
'Twas better to move than to sit and wait;
The late-come guest to the board will come,
But the loiterer loses his way in the wood.
All fires burn and soon grow cold,
All ale grows old and all meat grows stale,
The days pass hence and the deeds remain,
Thy fame shall live through the years of time.
A man should have self-esteem,
But not too much and never too high,
For the heart that knows its measure well
Shall be honored in the eyes of men.
I know that I hung on the windy tree,
Nine nights full long with never a wound,
While Odin my father and I did spin
The weaving of fate and threads divine.
From Yggdrasil fell I with sorrow sore,
Wounded by spear and bound with gold,
Yet never did I die from the wounds I got,
For magic words made me whole and sound.
I know the spells that no warrior knows,
That strengthen the heart of the battle-worn man,
I know how to make my enemy weak,
Though his sword be bright and his courage sure.
I know how to calm the stormy waves,
And hush the wind when it rages wild,
I know how to heal the sick in heart,
And ease the pain of the weary soul.
I know what strengthens the roots of the tree,
What wakens the stones from their silent sleep,
I know the words of great power that are hidden,
That hold the world from day to day.
I speak not of riches of bright-shining gold,
But of wisdom gained through the passing years,
For he who has wealth may lose it all,
But his mind shall live when his days are done.
The foolish man who goes to a feast,
Should mind his silence and think before speech,
For a single word of the unwise mouth
Can bring a man to his greatest shame.
Be cheerful and friendly, be kind to your kin,
Be fair in your dealings with all men alike,
Never mock the old or despise the poor,
For poverty comes to the highest born.
The fire warms the friend who sits at the hearth,
But a friend at need shall be gold to thee,
Keep faith with the oath that thy lips have sworn,
Or shame shall follow thee all thy days.
A man should be kind to his friends so dear,
And to his foes he should show disdain,
But he who goes about heaping up hate,
Shall find himself all alone at last.
The knowing man goes not forth in haste,
But thinks of the end before he acts,
For rash is the man who goes unprepared,
To battle or feast or a strangers' hall.
Cattle die and kinsmen die,
And so at last must thou die too,
But the deeds of a man shall never die,
If he lives in honor and truth.
No better burden a man can bear,
Than much good sense, and wisdom old,
No worse a bane than to have too much,
Of ale and drink at the board combined.
A staff in the hand as a sign of old age,
A good warm cloak when the wind blows cold,
A companion true at the side of the road,
These are the treasures a man holds dear.
In the house that is warm are kindly hearts,
And in the heart that is kind dwells peace,
But he who goes out with a grudging heart,
Shall find no welcome at any door.
The guest shall come and go in peace,
And leave behind him neither friend nor foe,
Be kind to the stranger who comes to thy hall,
For he may be greater than thou dost know.
